grip.value: Index and middle fingers laid across the seams at the open end of the horseshoe, the seam that faces away from the body. Thumb underneath on the leather. Held loose toward the fingertips.

source.label: Wikipedia, Four-seam fastball

grip.note: Paraphrased from the cited reference, not quoted. Corroborated by Wikipedia (Fastball) and eFastball.

gripDetails.value: Fingers cross the outward-facing horseshoe seam, pads resting where the two seams run closest together.

source.label: Wikipedia, Four-seam fastball

gripDetails.note: Wikipedia (Four-seam fastball) and (Fastball) agree: across the cross-seam that faces away from the body. eFastball corroborates.

gripDetails.value: Roughly a finger-width apart. Secondary instruction puts the gap near half an inch to an inch.

source.label: Plate Crate, Mastering the four-seam fastball

gripDetails.note: The weakest grip claim. Primary references prescribe no numeric gap, and Driveline treats spacing as athlete preference, from pressed-together to widened.

gripDetails.value: Thumb directly underneath the ball on smooth leather, its base sometimes overlaying a lower seam, centered between the two top fingers.

source.label: Wikipedia, Four-seam fastball

gripDetails.note: Strongly corroborated across Wikipedia (Four-seam fastball), Wikipedia (Fastball), and eFastball.

gripDetails.value: Held loosely toward the fingertips with a gap to the palm, the usual cue for a quick, clean release. Not universal.

gripDetails.note: Surfaced disagreement: eFastball also says to squeeze, and Driveline declines to prescribe pressure at all. Shown as the standard cue, not an absolute.

gripModel.palmGapCue: A little daylight stays between ball and palm so the ball leaves clean.
gripModel.releaseCue: Let the index and middle pads roll the ball off the top with backspin.

mechanics.value: Thrown over the top with the fingers behind the ball. The release rolls the ball from the fingertips with backspin so all four seams cross the oncoming air each revolution. That is the name: four seams biting the airflow per turn.

voice.value: A fastball could rise in principle, if you could get enough spin on it.

source.label: The Hardball Times, David Kagan citing Alan Nathan

voice.note: Alan Nathan, relayed by David Kagan in The Hardball Times. No human reaches the spin a literal rise would need, so a four-seam only drops less. It does not rise.

spinAxis.value: Near-horizontal backspin, the axis lying close to flat across the ball.

source.label: Wikipedia, Fastball

shape.value: Rides through the top of the zone. Backspin fights the fall the whole way, so it drops less than a spinless ball and looks like it holds its line. It carries; it never literally rises.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, vertical movement context

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. The carry is real; how far it carries depends on the arm.

teaching.value: Backspin throws a Magnus force upward, but it stays smaller than the ball weight, so the pitch drops less than a spinless one. It rides. It does not rise.

source.label: The Hardball Times, David Kagan citing Alan Nathan

teaching.note: Kagan, citing Nathan: the Magnus force runs a touch under the ball's weight, so the pitch falls less than a spinless one. The official MLB.com definition frames the same effect as induced rise, not literal rising.

masterVariants.context: The carry-ceiling case: a clean backspin read and a top-zone finish hitters keep swinging under.

distinction.value: A clean carry read taken to its ceiling. His four-seam plays at the top of the zone because hitters keep swinging under the ride.

source.label: MLB.com, Why Cole's fastball is in a league of its own (Statcast)

masterVariants.context: The carry case. Elite ride from a flat approach, the pitch hitters swing under.

distinction.value: Big ride from a low, flat slot. The ball stays on a flatter plane than the eye expects, so it finishes above where the swing is aimed — carry as a weapon at the top.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Spencer Strider

masterVariants.context: The axis-cleanup case. He raised his slot, cut the arm-side run, and converted that lost run into ride.

distinction.value: He raised his arm slot, traded away arm-side run, and converted it into pure ride — the same pitch, recut for carry.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Hunter Greene

guide.tagline: The pitch you trust for a strike. It takes the truest line and gives the hand the cleanest exit.
guide.feel: Least resistance. Loose in the fingertips, shallow in the hand. Let it sling cleanly off the pads.
steps: Lay your index and middle fingers flat across the wide part of the horseshoe seam, the part that faces away from you.
Keep them about a finger-width apart. Pressed close is fine; just do not spread them wide.
Rest your thumb underneath on smooth leather, centered below the two fingers.
Hold it out toward the fingertips with a little daylight to the palm. Stay loose so the hand adds the least resistance it can.

does.headline: It fights gravity longer than your eye expects.
does.plain: Clean backspin pushes up on the ball the whole way to the plate, so it falls less than a ball thrown with no spin. The hitter swings where they expect it, and it arrives a touch higher. That is carry. It rides; it never literally rises.

seam.equationPlain: x = 2 sin t + sin 3t,   y = 2 cos t - cos 3t,   z = 2√2 cos 2t
seam.parameterization: t from 0 to 2π, each point normalized to the ball radius, the whole curve rotated by the spin-axis quaternion.

source.label: Wikipedia, Baseball (ball)

stitchCount.note: A manufacturing convention, not a number written into the rulebook, which fixes only weight and circumference.

accuracyNote.note: The canonical degree-6 cover constants were not pinnable, so the documented figure-eight closed form stands in. The honest label is seam-informed schematic, never seam-accurate.

grip.value: Index and middle fingers run along the pair of narrow seams that sit close together, rather than across the horseshoe. That orientation tilts the ball away from pure backspin and trades ride for arm-side run and sink.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Sinker or Two-Seam Fastball

gripDetails.value: The standard Driveline two-seam grip lays the index and middle fingers on the two close seams, thumb underneath or slightly off-center, ring finger steadying the side. It is the baseline grip most arms reach for first.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Sinker or Two-Seam Fastball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. Driveline labels this the default two-seam family and the one most pitchers default to.

gripDetails.value: For a sinker specifically, the index finger often shifts slightly inward while keeping seam contact, biasing the release toward side-spin and diving action over balanced backspin-and-run.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Sinker or Two-Seam Fastball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased.

gripDetails.value: The most extreme movers ride a single seam: a one-seam grip that orients the leather to bend the airflow itself, adding run and sink beyond what the spin alone would suggest. Most of the biggest seam-shifted-wake movers Driveline studied were throwing a one-seam sinker.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, Seam-Shifted Wakes and their Effect on Sinkers

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. The one-seam grip dominated Driveline's study of the largest axis-deviation pitchers.

fingerPlacement.label: Index

mechanics.value: The release rolls the fingers over the outer half of the ball, adding side-spin to the backspin and tilting the axis toward the arm. A lower slot adds more of that tilt; on one-seam grips the seam orientation adds late sink without changing the slot. The cue is a downhill plane attacked at the knees, a pitch built to be hit on the ground.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Sinker or Two-Seam Fastball

mechanics.note: Paraphrased from Driveline; the seam-shifted-wake point is cross-referenced to the Driveline SSW article.

spinAxis.value: Backspin tilted toward the throwing arm, the axis rolled off the four-seam's near-vertical toward the hand. Less of the spin lifts the ball, so it rides less and sinks. Mirror the tilt for a left-hander.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Sinker or Two-Seam Fastball

spinAxis.note: General orientation, not a measured axis. A lower arm slot leans the tilt further; Valdez re-oriented his by dropping his arm angle.

shape.value: Hard arm-side run with late sink, finishing at the knees. It gives up the four-seam's ride and tails toward the throwing hand instead, so it appears to dive by comparison and rolls bats over into the ground. The extreme movers run on the seams, bending later than the spin alone would suggest.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Framber Valdez

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured break. The MLB glossary defines the sinker qualitatively by hard downward and arm-side movement; it sets no benchmark.

teaching.value: A four-seam rides because near-pure backspin points the Magnus force straight up. A sinker tilts that axis toward the arm and throws in less efficient spin, so the force points up and sideways instead. The ball rides less, runs toward the arm, and finishes low, which is how it turns barrels into ground balls.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Sinker (SI)

teaching.note: Synthesized from the MLB glossary (drops, forces grounders, thrown lower with less spin) and the Driveline axis and seam-shifted-wake mechanics.

display.heroIntro: Same arm, the axis rolled toward the hand. Ride becomes run, and the ball finishes at the knees. Taken to its extreme, the most movement and the least ride, this is the pitch the pros call a sinker.
display.foundationCaption: It rides far less than a four-seam and runs toward the arm. Thrown low, it produces ground balls.
display.mastersIntro: Three documented sinkers: the two-seam taken to its extreme, three ways down. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart lives in the read, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The textbook modern sinker as a ground-ball engine, a whole elite starter built around one arm-side-running, diving fastball thrown at the knees.

distinction.value: The ground-ball engine. Hard arm-side run and almost no ride leave the ball diving at the knees, and he has led the league in rolling bats over into weak contact — a whole rotation arm built around one sinker thrown low.

source.label: FanGraphs, Framber Valdez

masterVariants.context: The right-handed answer to the same idea: a sinker-first starter whose wide arm-side spread rolls bats over into weak contact.

distinction.value: The low-spin case taken to its end. He throws it with so little ride that it barely fights gravity at all, so it sinks hard and runs wide to the arm side — a sinker-first starter who manufactures ground balls in bulk.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Logan Webb

masterVariants.context: The seam-shifted-wake case: a power sinker whose movement bends well past what its spin axis predicts, the clearest live example of a ball running on its seams.

distinction.value: The seam-shifted-wake case. His power sinker bends well past where the spin axis says it should, the ball running late on its seams — among the most deceptive sinkers in the game because the movement arrives after the eye has committed.

source.label: Colin Hofmeister, Reverse-Engineering the Perfect Sinker

guide.tagline: Almost as hard as the four-seam, but it tails toward your arm and sinks. A ground-ball pitch.
guide.feel: Same effort as the four-seam. Stay behind it and let the seams do the work.
steps: Set your index and middle fingers along the two narrow seams where they run close together, not across the horseshoe.
Keep the same width and softness as your four-seam: a finger-width apart, held out in the hand.
Rest your thumb underneath on the leather; some pitchers ride the side of the thumb against a seam.
Throw it like a fastball. The grip makes the movement. You do not have to.

does.headline: It runs toward your arm and settles at the knees.
does.plain: Running along the seams tilts the spin, so the ball tails toward your throwing hand and rides far less than a four-seam, which is why it sinks by comparison. You trade a little pure life for movement that buys ground balls and weak contact.

grip.value: The hand gets on top of the ball so the fingers impart pure topspin. The middle finger leverages a seam, the index rests on the leather beside it, the thumb sits underneath on a seam, and the ball is tucked back toward the palm to load spin.

grip.note: Paraphrased from Driveline, not quoted. Spike and knuckle variants are a common feel-based alternative.

gripDetails.value: Driveline's standard curve grip leverages the middle finger on the seam with the index on the leather and the thumb on a seam underneath. Topspin comes from getting the fingers in front of the ball and pulling down, the cue being to yank it down with the middle finger.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Curveball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. Spike "CB 3-4" variants live on the same page.

gripDetails.value: The name is the clock face: the break runs in a straight line from 12 to 6. A higher arm slot makes it more vertical, a true 12-6; a lower slot tilts it toward a 1-7 slurve with more sideways movement.

source.label: Baseball Monkey, How to Throw a Curveball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased coaching reference for the clock-face naming and the arm-slot relationship.

mechanics.value: Topspin is generated by getting on top of the ball and driving the fingers over the front of it, pulling down so it rotates forward rather than with a slider's lateral tilt. Per Driveline, a higher release point leads to a more top-down shape, the classic 12-6, while lower slots add sideways run and turn it into a slurve.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Curveball

mechanics.note: Paraphrased from Driveline. The fastball-mirror framing is grounded in MLB.com vertical-movement context.

spinAxis.value: Near-pure topspin: the axis lies roughly flat and points the Magnus force straight down. Kershaw's curve, the cleanest modern 12-6, sits near the bottom of the clock with almost all of its spin doing work. More glove-side tilt makes a diagonal power curve instead.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Clayton Kershaw

spinAxis.note: Kershaw's curve reads as near-pure topspin from Savant's spin-direction table.

shape.value: A heavy, late, straight-down drop. Topspin and gravity pull the same way, so the floor falls out far harder than a spinless ball would, with little sideways run. The cleanest ones drop almost straight; tilt the axis glove-side and it sweeps into a diagonal power curve.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, vertical movement context

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. The curveball is the game's biggest-dropping pitch; the straight-down read is the signature of a true 12-6.

teaching.value: The curve is the four-seam fastball's mirror. The fastball spins backspin, so its Magnus force points up and it rides; the 12-6 spins topspin, so its Magnus force points down and it drops. Same physics, flipped axis: where the fastball fights the fall, the curve doubles it.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, vertical movement context

teaching.note: The mirror relationship: a backspun fastball carries, a topspun curveball drops, the same effect run in opposite directions.

display.heroIntro: Topspin instead of backspin, so the Magnus force points down and joins gravity. It falls straight, 12 to 6, far harder than a spinless ball would.
display.foundationCaption: Topspin points the Magnus force down. It drops far more than a spinless ball, with little sideways break.
display.mastersIntro: A textbook 12-6, the curve that anchored a career beside it, and a power curve to show where the straight-down line ends. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart is in the read, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The textbook pure 12-6: almost all of its movement is straight-down drop with minimal sideways run. MLB's vertical-movement context names him as a large-drop curveball thrower.

distinction.value: The straight-down standard. His curve falls almost on a vertical line, with so little sideways run that it reads as pure 12-to-6 — the drop everyone else's curve is measured against.

claim.value: Three-time Cy Young Award winner and a 2014 NL MVP.

He said it came out the same speed no matter how hard he threw it.

distinction.value: The curve as a career. A true straight-down 12-6 in his prime that flattened with age into a two-plane sweep, glove-side run creeping in as the slot dropped — the same pitch aging from a vertical drop toward a diagonal break.

guide.tagline: It falls straight off a table, twelve o’clock to six. Same arm speed, late and hard.
guide.feel: Get on top and pull down through the front of the ball. Stay over it; finish short.
steps: Run your middle finger along a seam and rest your thumb on the back seam, the ball pinched between them.
Let your index lie alongside with almost no pressure, or tuck its knuckle for a sharper bite.
Throw the front of the ball, not the back. That is what makes the topspin.
Keep fastball arm speed and pull down hard and short, like chopping straight down.

does.headline: Topspin drags it down off a cliff.
does.plain: Where the fastball spins backward, the curve spins forward. That topspin pulls the ball down late. It looks like a fastball out of the hand, then the floor drops out. A true twelve-to-six falls almost straight, with little sideways break.

grip.value: The thumb and index finger form an OK circle against the inner side of the ball while the other three fingers lay across the top. The ball rests deeper in the hand, which deadens the exit, and the off-center grip tilts the axis toward the arm for fade.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Changeup (CH)

grip.note: Paraphrased from MLB.com Glossary, not quoted.

gripDetails.value: MLB.com calls the circle change the most common changeup grip: thumb and index in a circle on the inner side, the other three fingers over the rest of the ball. Held farther back in the hand, sometimes toward the palm, which is what takes the speed off.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Changeup (CH)

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased from the official glossary.

gripDetails.value: Driveline cues the fade by rolling the hand over the ball or swiping its inside: the more the hand turns over, the more arm-side run. The more the axis leans toward the arm, the more the ball runs that way.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Changeup

gripDetails.note: Mechanical cues paraphrased from Driveline.

gripDetails.value: The deception is the late arrival on identical arm action, so the standard cue is to keep fastball arm speed and let the grip deaden the pitch, not the throw.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Changeup (CH)

gripDetails.note: The "start the swing before the pitch arrives" deception is the glossary definition; the maintain-arm-speed cue is standard and widely taught.

fingerPlacement.label: Index

mechanics.value: The arm matches the fastball; the deeper grip and loosened circle deaden the exit, not a softer throw. A slight pronation at release tilts the axis arm-side, so the ball fades toward the throwing hand and tumbles as it crosses.

spinAxis.value: A fastball-like backspin rotated toward the arm. The axis leans arm-side rather than pointing straight up, and the harder it leans, the harder the ball fades toward the throwing hand.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Changeup

spinAxis.note: Where the tilt sits depends on how the hand turns over; observed and spin-based reads of the axis differ by method.

shape.value: It runs toward the throwing hand and tumbles under the barrel, late and on the arm-side. The fade is real and the drop is real; how wide it runs depends on how hard the arm turns the axis over.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Logan Webb

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. The arm-side run and the drop are both genuine; the amount varies arm to arm.

teaching.value: The late arrival and the fade work because the arm action is identical to the fastball. The eyes read fastball, the swing starts on fastball timing, then the ball arrives a beat late while fading down and toward the arm, and the barrel passes over it. Take the arm speed off and the deception collapses.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Changeup (CH)

teaching.note: Synthesizes the MLB.com deception definition with Driveline mechanics. The whole pitch lives on the gap between the look and the clock.

voice.value: It's just an outlier pitch. The spin I'm able to create makes it different from every other changeup.

source.label: MLB.com, Devin Williams and the Airbender changeup

voice.note: Devin Williams to The Athletic in 2020, re-published in MLB.com's Airbender explainer. It speaks to his own changeup, the extreme tail of the family, not the circle change in general.

display.heroIntro: The same arm action, the ball deeper in the hand. It arrives late, fading toward the arm and tumbling under the barrel while the swing is already gone.
display.foundationCaption: It fades toward the arm and drops under a fastball-timed swing, all on identical arm speed.
display.mastersIntro: Four documented changeups, four answers. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart is in the read, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The textbook circle change and one of the defining changeups of his generation, a bat-missing pitch built on identical arm action and late arrival.

distinction.value: The bat-missing standard. Hitters chased it because the arm action read fastball and the ball was simply gone by the time the barrel arrived — the textbook everyone since has measured against.

source.label: FanGraphs, The Best Pitches of 2011: Changeup (Cole Hamels)

masterVariants.context: The extreme tail, the Airbender. We show it as the outer limit of the circle grip while flagging that its movement pushes toward screwball territory.

distinction.value: The outer limit of the grip. His Airbender fades so hard and so late that it reads less like a changeup than a screwball — the same family, taken past where the family usually ends.

source.label: MLB.com, Devin Williams and the Airbender changeup

masterVariants.context: The other archetype: a high-volume shape-and-location change built for weak contact and ground balls, not empty swings, the counterweight to the bat-missers.

distinction.value: The ground-ball answer. His change wins on shape and location instead of a huge timing gap, beating the ball into the dirt rather than past the bat — proof a great changeup need not be a chase pitch.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Logan Webb

masterVariants.context: The historical benchmark, and the honest one. Widely called the best changeup of its era, built on identical arm action rather than raw movement; the record shows the disguise did the work. He has his own chapter in the Craftsmen.

distinction.value: The deception case, fully honest. The legend was the speed drop; the more useful lesson is the disguise — same arm, same look, late arrival.

source.label: FanGraphs, Johan Santana Rides Changeup to No-Hitter

guide.tagline: It looks exactly like the fastball and arrives a beat slower. It wrecks a hitter’s timing.
guide.feel: Fastball arm speed, always. More surface area and deeper hand contact take the speed off, not a slower arm.
steps: Make an OK sign: curl your index down to meet your thumb on the side of the ball, forming a visible circle.
Let the ball sit deeper in your hand, toward the palm, with more surface on the ball than a fastball.
Throw it with fastball arm speed and the same release; the deep grip deadens the pitch for you.
Let the palm turn out toward your arm side at release. Never try to spin it by hand.

does.headline: It lies. Same look, slower clock.
does.plain: Buried in the hand, the ball comes out slower with a little arm-side fade. The hitter has already committed to fastball timing, so the swing starts early, and the barrel passes over the top of it.

grip.value: The index and middle fingers are wedged to opposite sides of the ball, spread wide on the outside of the leather, wider and farther back than a forkball, with the ball coming off the inside of the fingers rather than the fingertips. Thrown with the arm action of a fastball.

source.label: Wikipedia, Split-finger fastball

grip.note: Paraphrased from Wikipedia (Split-finger fastball) and corroborated by Driveline; not quoted.

gripDetails.value: A descendant of the forkball, the splitter is held farther forward in the hand with a wider split between the fingers, which is why it can be thrown harder than the deep-jammed forkball.

source.label: Wikipedia, Split-finger fastball

gripDetails.note: Wikipedia describes the splitter as a faster, shallower relative of the forkball.

gripDetails.value: Driveline files the splitter under the same family and notes its defining trait: it spins far less than a fastball. The lost backspin, not any added force, is what makes it drop.

source.label: Driveline Baseball Help, Pitch Grips: Splitters

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased from Driveline. Described as a loss of spin, in words, not a measured figure.

gripDetails.value: Bruce Sutter's unusually large hands let him spread the split far enough to execute it; he learned it from instructor Fred Martin after his fastball faded post-surgery and never changed the grip after the first day.

source.label: National Baseball Hall of Fame, Sutter remembered as pioneer of the split-finger

gripDetails.note: From the Hall of Fame tribute to Sutter; the hand-size detail is corroborated on his Wikipedia page.

fingerPlacement.label: Index
fingerPlacement.note: Wedged to the outside of the ball, well off the middle finger.

fingerPlacement.label: Middle
fingerPlacement.note: Wedged to the opposite side, the wide split that kills the spin.

fingerPlacement.label: Thumb
fingerPlacement.note: Underneath on the leather, centered between the split fingers.

source.label: Wikipedia, Split-finger fastball

provenance.note: Outside the seams is the splitter tell; depth and spread vary with hand size, and short fingers genuinely struggle to hold it.

gripModel.palmGapCue: The ball sits up between the split fingers, farther forward than a forkball.
gripModel.releaseCue: Throw it like the fastball and let the wide split deaden the spin.

mechanics.value: It is thrown with the arm speed and release of a fastball. The wide split and a stiffer wrist dramatically reduce the backspin, weakening the Magnus lift that keeps a four-seamer riding, so gravity wins late and the ball drops sharply, the classic trap-door look.

source.label: The Conversation, the physics of Kevin Gausman's splitter

mechanics.note: Paraphrased from The Conversation's physics breakdown of Gausman's splitter.

voice.value: I’d like to tell you I worked and worked at it but I’d be lying to you because it did come to me right away. The first day I threw it I’d get it to break ... I never adjusted my grip after the first day.

source.label: National Baseball Hall of Fame, Sutter remembered as pioneer of the split-finger

voice.note: Bruce Sutter, recalling learning the split-finger from Fred Martin, in the Hall of Fame tribute.

spinAxis.value: A fastball-like backspin axis out of the hand, so the two look identical at release; the deception lives in matching the fastball's tilt until the pitch separates in flight.

source.label: Wikipedia, Split-finger fastball

shape.value: Rides the fastball's line out of the hand, then falls off the table late and hard, a trap-door drop that arrives just under the barrel. With the backspin stripped away it tumbles instead of carrying, and the floor seems to vanish at the last instant.

source.label: The Conversation, the physics of Kevin Gausman's splitter

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. The fall is real; how much depends on the arm and the hand.

teaching.value: Because it leaves the hand looking like the fastball, the hitter starts a fastball swing; then the lost backspin lets gravity pull it under the barrel at the last instant. Take the split away or put the backspin back and it just becomes a slow fastball.

source.label: The Conversation, the physics of Kevin Gausman's splitter

teaching.note: Synthesized from The Conversation and Wikipedia: the weakened-Magnus drop off a fastball look is the whole pitch.

display.heroSub: A fastball look, a trap-door drop.
display.heroIntro: Wedge the fingers wide, throw it like the fastball, and let the lost backspin drop it off the table at the plate. The same arm, the same path, then the floor falls out.
display.foundationCaption: It barely rides, so it falls almost like a spinless ball, late and under the barrel.
display.mastersIntro: From the pitch's pioneer to its modern swing-and-miss kings. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each arm's splitter apart is in the read and the hand, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The pioneer. Taught the split-finger by instructor Fred Martin after arm surgery, he rode it to the Hall of Fame as one of the first dominant closers.

distinction.value: The first to ride this pitch to the Hall of Fame, and the proof of concept for the whole grip — a closer who turned one trap-door drop into a career after his fastball left him.

source.label: Wikipedia, Bruce Sutter

masterVariants.context: Added the splitter in the nineties, nicknamed it "Mr. Splitty," and made it his out pitch, one of the most devastating in the pitch's history. He has his own chapter in the Craftsmen.

distinction.value: Late-career reinvention in one pitch: he bolted "Mr. Splitty" onto a power arsenal and kept missing barrels long after the pure heat would have faded, which is why he stayed an ace into his forties.

source.label: Wikipedia, Roger Clemens

masterVariants.context: The modern volume king. His splitter has produced more swinging strikes than any other pitcher's since the Statcast era began, by a wide margin.

distinction.value: The most prolific swing-and-miss splitter of the tracked era, and not close — he throws it like a fastball over and over and hitters keep waving under the late drop because the look never tips.

source.label: Jays Journal, Gausman's splitter is one of the best pitches in baseball

masterVariants.context: The unhittable one. His trap-door splitter became, in a single season, about as unhittable as a pitch gets.

distinction.value: A splitter thrown hard enough to look like premium heat until the floor drops — for one season hitters who put a swing on it almost never made contact, which is as close to unhittable as the pitch comes.

source.label: MLB.com, Ohtani's splitter still the most unhittable pitch

guide.tagline: It looks like the fastball the whole way, then the floor drops out at the plate.
guide.feel: Throw it as hard as the fastball. The wide split does the work, not a softer arm.
steps: Wedge your index and middle fingers wide, to opposite sides of the ball, outside the seam tracks rather than directly on them.
Let the ball sit up between them, farther forward than a deep forkball.
Rest your thumb underneath, centered between the split fingers.
Throw it with fastball arm speed and a firm wrist; the split kills the spin for you.

does.headline: A fastball that quits on the hitter.
does.plain: The wide grip strips the backspin a fastball lives on, so the ball stops riding and gravity takes it down late. The hitter has already swung where the fastball should be, and the pitch falls under the barrel.

grip.value: A four-seam grip with both fingers shifted slightly toward one side of the ball — the glove side for the handedness — and thrown like a fastball. That off-center placement produces a short cutting movement at the plate without a slider's pronounced finger pull.

source.label: MLB.com Glossary — Cutter (FC)

grip.note: Paraphrased. MLB.com: both fingers placed toward one side (the right of the ball for a righty, the left for a lefty) to produce the cutting movement.

gripDetails.value: A common build is a four-seam fastball grip with the ball set slightly off center in the hand — the same fastball, just nudged off the middle.

source.label: Wikipedia, Cut fastball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased from Wikipedia's grip description.

gripDetails.value: The cut comes from that finger placement and a touch of supination at release, not from a big wrist action — it is a fastball that bites. Lean harder into the supination and the off-center grip and it drifts toward hard-slider territory.

source.label: MLB.com Glossary — Cutter (FC)

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. MLB confirms the grip-driven cut and fastball delivery; the supination/slider-drift framing is coaching-standard.

gripDetails.value: It is the most ride-or-die-able single pitch in the game: Mariano Rivera threw almost nothing but his cutter across his whole career, and Kenley Jansen leans on his nearly as hard — proof that one great cutter can be a whole career.

source.label: Wikipedia, Cut fastball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. Rivera and Jansen are among the most cutter-reliant arms ever tracked.

fingerPlacement.label: Index
fingerPlacement.note: Across the horseshoe like a four-seam, both fingers shifted slightly toward the glove side of the ball.

fingerPlacement.label: Middle
fingerPlacement.note: Beside the index, carrying a touch more pressure to set the cut.

fingerPlacement.label: Thumb
fingerPlacement.note: Underneath, centered, as on a four-seam.

orientation.palmFacing: Palm to the plate — the shift in the grip, not the wrist, makes the cut.

provenance.value: Finger placement solved as an off-center four-seam: pads still crossing the horseshoe seam, the pair displaced toward the glove side, middle finger carrying the pressure. Schematic geometry, not an athlete scan.

provenance.note: MLB.com: both fingers set toward one side of the ball; the cut comes from placement and a touch of supination, never a slider-style pull.

gripModel.thumbRole: Thumb stays centered underneath, as on the four-seam it is built from.
gripModel.palmGapCue: Held like a fastball, not buried — the cut is from the grip, not from squeezing.
gripModel.releaseCue: Throw it like a four-seam with a hair of supination — let the ball cut off the fingers rather than pulling down the side like a slider.
gripModel.visualCaveat: Grip geometry is schematic; the more a pitcher supinates and shifts off-center, the closer it drifts to a hard slider.

mechanics.value: It is thrown a hair softer than the four-seam, with the same arm action, so it tunnels off the fastball. The late glove-side cut comes from the off-center grip and a slightly supinated release rather than from a slider's downward finger pull.

source.label: MLB.com Glossary — Cutter (FC)

mechanics.note: Paraphrased. The fastball-minus feel and same-arm-action tunneling are coaching-standard, anchored to the MLB glossary's fastball-with-a-cut framing.

spinAxis.value: A fastball axis tilted toward the glove side, just shy of upright for a right-hander, with a little of the spin turned gyro. That mix is why it rides like a fastball but cuts instead of running true.

source.label: MLB.com Glossary — Cutter (FC)

spinAxis.note: The tilt is the coaching reference, not a glossary quote; MLB frames the cutter as a fastball that moves glove-side.

shape.value: Rides almost like a four-seam, then bites short and late to the glove side at the very end — a small, sudden cut, far shorter than a slider's sweep. The harder a pitcher leans on it, the more ride he trades for that bite.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Emmanuel Clase

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. The cut is real and late; how far it bites depends on the arm — some ride high and fastball-like, some cut harder and shorter.

teaching.value: A four-seam rides on pure backspin: the axis is sideways to flight, so the Magnus force points straight up and the ball carries. Tilt that axis toward the glove side and let a little of the spin go gyro, and the ball trades some of its ride for a short, late cut to the glove side — a fastball that bites just enough to miss the barrel.

source.label: MLB.com Glossary — Cutter (FC)

teaching.note: Synthesis of the four-seam Magnus model and the MLB glossary's fastball-with-a-cut description.

display.heroIntro: Take a four-seam grip, shift it slightly off-center, and throw it like a fastball. The ball rides almost like a heater, then cuts to the glove side at the last moment — just enough to find the end of the bat instead of the barrel. Rivera built a career on one.
display.foundationCaption: It rides like a fastball but its axis is tilted glove-side, so a little of the lift becomes a short, late cut.
display.mastersIntro: Three cutters across eras: the archetype, the modern extreme, and the cutter as a primary fastball. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart is in the read, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The archetype: one cutter, thrown almost every pitch, all the way to the Hall of Fame. The proof that a single great pitch can be a whole career.

distinction.value: A modest, almost gentle cut — but so late and so repeatable that hitters who knew it was coming still could not square it. The whole career rode on one pitch that bit just enough, every single time.

source.label: Bleacher Report, Visual Breakdown of the Rise & Dominance of Mariano Rivera's Cutter

masterVariants.context: The modern extreme: a cutter thrown two of every three pitches, with a high-ride shape that behaves more like a fastball than a breaking ball.

distinction.value: The cutter pushed to fastball intent, riding high and biting hard at once — so heavy and so firm that it plays like a heater that happens to cut, and hitters have to honor it like a true breaking ball.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Emmanuel Clase

masterVariants.context: The cutter as a primary fastball, leaned on hard enough to anchor a Cy Young arsenal — a shorter, harder-to-square shape than Clase's.

distinction.value: A shorter, tighter cut than Clase's, leaned on as the everyday fastball — the bite is small and the ride is honest, which is exactly what makes it so hard to barrel across a whole start.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Corbin Burnes

guide.tagline: A fastball thrown off-center, so it rides like a heater and then bites to the glove side at the end.
guide.feel: Throw it like your fastball. Shift the grip slightly off-center and let the ball cut off your fingers — do not pull down the side like a slider.
steps: Start from your four-seam grip and shift both fingers slightly toward the glove side of the ball.
Let the middle finger carry a touch more pressure.
Throw it with full fastball arm speed and just a hair of supination.
Let the ball cut off the fingers; the bite is small and late, and that is the point.

does.headline: It rides like a fastball, then jumps to the glove side at the end.
does.plain: A cutter looks like a fastball almost the whole way, then darts to the glove side right before it gets to the plate. That tiny, late move is enough to catch the thin part of the bat instead of the barrel — which is why a great one breaks so many bats.

gripDetails.value: Bullet spin comes from a slightly supinated hand pulling down the side of the ball so it spirals like a football. A spiked grip, with the index finger knuckled, helps the ball slip off the middle finger to maximize the gyro and minimize back or side spin.

source.label: Driveline Baseball Help, Pitch Grips: Sliders

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. The throw-it-like-a-football cue and the spiked-grip mechanism are Driveline's.

gripDetails.value: The red dot is the visible signature of the gyro spin: as the ball spirals, the seams trace a dot on the leading face. A tighter dot means more useful spin; a perfectly gyroscopic, no-dot slider is the flat, hittable cement mixer.

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. Hitters key on this dot to read slider from fastball.

mechanics.value: It lives in the gap between the fastball and the curve, a touch softer than the four-seam, thrown with a firmer wrist and a side-of-the-ball finger pull rather than a curve's over-the-top snap. The arm mimics the fastball; the late, short glove-side bite comes from gyro-dominant spin and from gravity acting on a ball with little Magnus lift.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Slider

mechanics.note: Paraphrased. The fastball-adjacent timing read is corroborated by MLB.com's slider framing.

spinAxis.value: The axis points largely toward the plate, bullet spin, so much of the spin is gyroscopic and does no Magnus work. Tracking reads that as low active spin: most of the turn is wasted as a spiral. For a right-hander the tilt sits late in the clock, biased to the glove side.

source.label: The Hardball Times, The Physics of the Gyro Pitch (Alan Nathan)

spinAxis.note: Axis-toward-plate physics from the Hardball Times gyro analysis. Described as orientation, not a measured figure.

shape.value: Stays on the fastball line, then bends late and short to the glove side, a sudden glove-side cut rather than a long sweep. It barely rides and barely tumbles; gravity, not lift, drives most of its fall. The hitter reads a red dot and commits a beat too soon.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Pitch Movement Leaderboard (Sliders)

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. How far it bends depends on the arm and the grip; a tight gyro slider is short and sharp, a sweeper-leaning one is broad.

teaching.value: A four-seam spins backspin, its axis sideways to flight, so the Magnus force pushes up and it rides. A gyro slider spins like a thrown football, its axis pointed at the catcher, so the spin makes almost no Magnus force. With little lift to fight gravity it drops more than the fastball, and the small tilt left in the axis bends it glove-side, late and short. Same arm, opposite Magnus budget.

source.label: The Hardball Times, The Physics of the Gyro Pitch (Alan Nathan)

teaching.note: Synthesis of the active-spin definition and the Hardball Times gyro physics: an axis toward the plate produces no Magnus force.

display.heroSub: Spun like a football. Read by the late bite, not by a gun.
display.heroIntro: The axis points at the plate, so the spin does almost no work. It stays on the fastball line, then breaks late and short to the glove side. The hitter sees a red dot.
display.foundationCaption: Bullet spin means almost no Magnus lift, so it drops more than the fastball and bends late, glove-side.
display.mastersIntro: Two reference gyro sliders, then the sweeper boundary beside them. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart is in the shape and the read, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The reference-grade modern gyro slider: thrown at high volume, bullet-spin dominant, the textbook tight-dot shape and a top swing-and-miss weapon.

distinction.value: The textbook tight dot. Almost pure gyro, so it barely deflects in either plane and simply vanishes late, glove-side; hitters keep swinging through the spot where it used to be. The cleanest version of what the pitch is.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Dylan Cease

distinction.note: Described as shape and read, not a gauge. A near-pure gyro slider shows the tightest red dot and the shortest, latest bite.

masterVariants.context: The purest gyro of the set, thrown firm and tight. It shows what almost-all-gyro looks like: barely any early deflection, then late chase bite.

distinction.value: The tightest, flattest gyro of the three. Almost none of the spin does Magnus work, so it neither rides nor sweeps, it just holds the fastball line and bites a hair at the end. Deception carries it where movement does not.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Tyler Glasnow

distinction.note: Described as shape and read, not a gauge. The near-pure gyro profile means minimal deflection in either plane.

masterVariants.context: The boundary case, included honestly, not as a gyro exemplar. The tracking taxonomy called it a slider, but its shape is sweeper-like. It marks where the gyro slider ends and the sweeper begins.

distinction.value: Not really a gyro at all, and that is the point. From his low slot it does real sidespin work and sweeps broad and tumbling across the zone, where the gyro sliders cut short and flat. It marks the edge of the family.

source.label: Baseball Savant (Statcast), Chris Sale

distinction.note: Described as shape and read, not a gauge. Its sidespin-heavy, sweeping shape sits at the boundary between the gyro slider and the sweeper.

guide.tagline: It looks like the fastball, then breaks late and short to the glove side. The hitter sees a red dot.
guide.feel: Firm wrist. Stay on the side of the ball and let it slide off. Think of throwing a tight spiral.
steps: Set your index and middle fingers close together, just off-center toward the outer third of the ball.
Ride the middle finger on a seam, bias the pressure to the index side, thumb supporting underneath.
Keep the hand slightly behind the ball and let it slip off the fingers like a thrown football.
Use your fastball arm; the late, short break comes from the spin, not from muscling it.

does.headline: It spins like a thrown football, so it barely fights gravity.
does.plain: Most of a slider’s spin points straight at the catcher, so it does almost no lifting work. With little to hold it up, it drops more than the fastball, and the small tilt left in the spin bends it glove-side: late, short, and hard to read until it is too late.

grip.value: A slider grip rotated further around the side of the ball. The index and middle fingers sit toward the outer third, the hand "gets around" the ball at release rather than cutting down through it, and a wider arm path lets the fingers sweep across the outside. That tilts the spin axis toward pure sidespin.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Sweeper (ST)

grip.note: Paraphrased. MLB.com: the sweeper is thrown with side-spin, "getting around" the ball, or with a two-seam grip — the gyro slider, by contrast, is thrown with bullet spin.

gripDetails.value: MLB.com frames the sweeper grip as side-spin first: the pitcher gets around the outside of the ball, or uses a two-seam orientation, where a traditional gyro slider is thrown with bullet spin that spirals like a football.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Sweeper (ST)

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased from the glossary entry, not quoted.

gripDetails.value: It is, mechanically, a slider family member — Driveline's slider grips, with the fingers set off-center and the ball allowed to roll from the hand, are the same starting point; the sweeper just rotates the hand further around the side for lateral spin.

source.label: Driveline Baseball, How to Throw a Slider

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. The off-center grip and let-it-slide-off cue are Driveline's; the further-around rotation is the sweeper variation.

gripDetails.value: A big-league pitcher will often carry two distinct slider grips — one for the sweeper, one for the gyro slider — and pick by intent: the sweeper to sweep a same-handed hitter off the plate, the gyro slider to bury one with a late, short bite.

source.label: MLB.com, Explaining the sweeper (slider vs sweeper break)

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. MLB.com's explainer cites a pitcher describing his two separate slider grips.

spinAxis.value: Tilted toward pure sidespin: the axis lies nearly sideways for a right-hander, almost flat across the ball, so the spin does its Magnus work across the side. That is the sweeper's whole identity and the opposite of the gyro slider, whose axis points at the catcher and spins like a thrown football.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Sweeper (ST)

spinAxis.note: MLB.com states the side-spin-vs-bullet-spin contrast that drives the axis difference. Described in words, no clock figure.

shape.value: A wide, mostly flat sweep to the glove side. It neither rides like a four-seam nor tumbles like a curve — almost all of its movement runs sideways, with little drop beyond gravity. It looks like a fastball, then runs off the plate well before it arrives.

source.label: MLB.com, Explaining the sweeper (slider vs sweeper break)

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. MLB.com defines the sweeper by its big horizontal movement with little vertical; how far it sweeps depends on the arm.

teaching.value: A gyro slider points its spin axis at the catcher and spirals like a football, so the spin makes almost no Magnus force and the pitch breaks late and short. A sweeper tilts that axis toward pure sidespin, so the same Magnus force that lifts a four-seam now pushes sideways — a wide, glove-side sweep with little drop. Same family as the slider, opposite Magnus budget.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Sweeper (ST)

teaching.note: Synthesis of MLB.com's side-spin-vs-bullet-spin contrast and the Magnus framing the atlas uses throughout.

display.heroIntro: Take a slider grip and get further around the side of the ball. The axis tilts toward pure sidespin, and the same force that lifts a fastball now pushes it sideways: a wide, glove-side sweep with little drop. The defining pitch of the last few seasons.
display.foundationCaption: Sidespin, not bullet spin. The Magnus force points sideways, so it sweeps wide and flat instead of biting late like a gyro slider.
display.mastersIntro: Three reference sweepers, three ways to win with the side-spin sweep. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart lives in the read, how the arm shapes the sweep, not a gauge.

distinction.value: The widest, most violent sweep in the game, and never the same shape two years running — he keeps recutting it, hunting more reach off the side. The benchmark every other sweeper gets measured against by eye.

source.label: MLB.com, Shohei Ohtani's 2025 pitching debut — a Statcast breakdown

distinction.note: MLB.com calls it maybe the best sweeper in the Majors and documents his year-to-year reshaping. Described as shape, no measured gauge.

guide.tagline: A slider grip turned around the side of the ball, so it sweeps wide and flat to the glove side instead of biting late.
guide.feel: Stay on the side of the ball and sweep your fingers around the outside, like spinning a frisbee. Not a downward pull.
steps: Start from your slider grip: index and middle on the outer third of the ball.
Rotate your hand further around the side, so you are getting around the ball, not on top of it.
Use a slightly wider arm path and let the fingers sweep across the outside at release.
Throw it with fastball intent; the wide break comes from the sidespin, not from muscling it.

does.headline: It spins on its side, so the air pushes it sideways.
does.plain: A sweeper spins on its side, axis lying flat across the ball instead of pointing at the catcher. That turns the same air force that lifts a fastball into a sideways push, so the ball sweeps a long way to the glove side and barely drops. It looks like a fastball, then runs off the plate well before it gets there.

grip.value: The ball is jammed deep between the index and middle fingers — forked wider than a splitter, wedged down toward the knuckles — and released with a downward snap of the wrist. That deep grip and snap deaden the spin so it tumbles down sharply, like a 12-to-6 curve.

It is common in Japan and seldom adopted in the majors, where it has a reputation as an arm-taxing pitch.

gripModel.releaseCue: Snap the wrist down as the ball squirts out from between the fingers; the deep grip kills the spin.
gripModel.visualCaveat: Grip geometry is schematic; the deep, wide fork demands extreme finger flexibility and is hard on the arm.

contacts.label: Index fork
contacts.seamRelation: Split wide, jammed deep along the inside of the finger
contacts.pressureRole: One side of the deep fork

contacts.label: Middle fork
contacts.seamRelation: Split wider than a splitter, wedged to the knuckles
contacts.pressureRole: Other side of the deep fork

contacts.label: Thumb
contacts.seamRelation: Underneath as support

mechanics.value: It is thrown with a fastball arm action, but the ball squirts out from between the deep-forked fingers with almost no spin rather than being spun. The downward wrist snap that gives it its tumble also puts real torque on the elbow, which is why MLB.com calls it one of the more taxing pitches to throw.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Forkball (FO)

mechanics.note: Paraphrased. The squirt-out release and the taxing-on-the-arm point are from the MLB glossary.

spinAxis.value: Very little spin, so no strong axis. What there is leans toward topspin tilted arm-side — consistent with a pitch that tumbles down with a touch of arm-side run.

source.label: FanGraphs, A (Ghost) Fork in the Road: Senga's Premier Pitch

spinAxis.note: The faint axis on a near-spinless ball leans toward a downward, arm-side tumble.

shape.value: A heavy, late, almost straight-down tumble — like the floor dropping out at the last instant. With almost no spin there is nothing to hold it up, so it falls off the table, and a modern ghost fork can fade a little arm-side on the way down. The drop is the pitch.

source.label: Baseball Prospectus, Spinning Yarn: The Forkball

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. The tumble is the read; how far it falls depends on the arm. The arm-side fade is a modern ghost-fork variation.

teaching.value: Most pitches fight gravity: backspin makes a Magnus force that holds the ball up. The forkball does the opposite. Jamming the ball deep and snapping it out kills the spin, so there is almost no lift, and gravity tumbles the ball down hard and late. The price for that free fall is torque on the arm.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Forkball (FO)

teaching.note: Synthesis of the low-spin grip and the MLB glossary's downward-snap / taxing description.

display.heroIntro: Wedge the ball all the way down between two split fingers, wider than a splitter, and snap the wrist as it squirts out. It leaves with almost no spin, so there is nothing to hold it up — gravity takes over and it tumbles down late. The catch: it is hard on the arm.
display.foundationCaption: Almost no spin means almost no lift, so gravity dominates: the ball plots near the spinless ball and falls off the table.
display.mastersIntro: Three forkballs: the modern ghost fork, the deadest-spin version anyone has thrown, and the arm that brought the pitch to the majors. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart is in the read, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The modern ace forkball — the "ghost fork" — a low-spin tumble with enough arm-side life to be a primary out-pitch.

distinction.value: The "ghost fork" — a dead-spin tumble he gave just enough arm-side fade to use as a primary out-pitch. The reshaping that added that fade may also be making it a touch easier for hitters to pick up: a sharper, straighter drop is harder to read than one that drifts.

source.label: FanGraphs, A (Ghost) Fork in the Road: Senga's Premier Pitch

masterVariants.context: The deadest-spin non-knuckleball forkball anyone has thrown — so little rotation it behaves almost like a knuckleball off a fastball arm.

distinction.value: So nearly spinless it acts like a knuckleball thrown with fastball intent: nothing holds it up, so it falls off the table about as hard as a non-knuckleball pitch can, with only a small, unpredictable drift to either side. The straightest, heaviest tumble in the wing.

source.label: MLB.com, Roki Sasaki's forkball and splitter explained

masterVariants.context: The "Tornado": the forkball that broke into the majors from Japan in 1995 and led both leagues in strikeouts on opposite sides of the Pacific.

distinction.value: The arm that sold the pitch to the majors — a deep, tumbling fork hidden behind a whirling, back-to-the-plate windup that led a league in strikeouts on each side of the Pacific. His version won on deception as much as drop.

source.label: Society for American Baseball Research, Hideo Nomo (BioProject)

guide.tagline: Jammed deep between two fingers and snapped out with no spin, so gravity tumbles it down late.
guide.feel: Bury the ball deep between your fingers, wider than a split. Snap your wrist down as it squirts out. Expect it to feel hard on the arm.
steps: Split your index and middle fingers wide and wedge the ball deep, toward the base of the fingers.
Keep your thumb underneath to hold it in place.
Throw with fastball arm speed and snap the wrist down as the ball leaves.
Let it squirt out with as little spin as possible — the less it spins, the harder it tumbles.

does.headline: It leaves with no spin, so it just falls.
does.plain: A forkball is squeezed so deep between the fingers that it comes out almost without spinning. With no spin there is nothing holding it up, so it tumbles down late and hard, like the floor dropped out. It fools hitters who are geared up for a fastball. The snap that makes it work is tough on the arm.

grip.value: The fingernails (or, for smaller hands, the bent knuckles) dig into the leather while the thumb braces underneath. The ball is held off the palm and pushed toward the plate with no wrist snap, so it leaves the hand with almost no rotation. Killing the spin is the entire goal.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Knuckleball (KN)

grip.note: Paraphrased. MLB.com: knuckles on the ball or hovering over it while the fingernails dig into the surface; the goal is to eliminate almost all spin.

gripDetails.value: MLB.com names the grip for the no-spin goal: knuckles on or just over the ball with the fingernails dug into the surface, thrown to eliminate almost all rotation so the ball flutters unpredictably.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Knuckleball (KN)

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased from the glossary, not quoted.

gripDetails.value: In practice most throwers use the fingertips and nails rather than true knuckles, with the thumb resting underneath for balance — young pitchers with smaller hands are the ones who actually use their knuckles.

source.label: Wikipedia, Knuckleball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. The fingertip-vs-knuckle distinction and the thumb-for-balance point are Wikipedia's, not the MLB glossary's.

gripDetails.value: The textbook target is to barely let the ball turn at all over the whole flight from hand to plate; the slowly changing seam position is what makes the path wander.

source.label: Wikipedia, Knuckleball

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. The barely-any-rotation target is Wikipedia's.

fingerPlacement.label: Index nail
fingerPlacement.note: Fingernail dug into the leather just behind a seam, knuckle bent.

fingerPlacement.label: Middle nail
fingerPlacement.note: Second fingernail beside the first; together they brace, not grip.

fingerPlacement.label: Thumb
fingerPlacement.note: Underneath for balance, so the ball can be pushed flat off the nails.

orientation.knuckleLine: Knuckles bent up and forward so only the nails meet the leather.
orientation.palmFacing: Palm flat to the plate through a push release — no snap, no roll.

provenance.value: Finger placement solved from the cited descriptions: index and middle fingernails dug in just behind a seam with the knuckles bent, thumb balancing beneath, the ball held off the palm. Schematic geometry, not an athlete scan.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Knuckleball (KN)

provenance.note: Some throwers brace with bent knuckles instead of nails; the no-spin push is the constant.

gripModel.thumbRole: Thumb sits underneath for balance while the fingernails hold the ball off the palm.
gripModel.palmGapCue: Ball held off the palm on the fingernails, never buried — the palm must not add spin.
gripModel.releaseCue: Push the ball flat toward the plate and let it leave with as little rotation as possible. No wrist snap.
gripModel.visualCaveat: Grip geometry is schematic; some throwers use bent knuckles instead of nails, and small hands change the placement.

contacts.pressureRole: Braces the ball to kill spin

mechanics.value: It is thrown soft, with an even, low-effort push instead of a hard arm whip, because arm speed adds the spin the pitch is trying to avoid. A firmer knuckleball trades some flutter for less reaction time, and many throwers carry two speeds.

source.label: FanGraphs, Tracking R.A. Dickey's Knuckleball

mechanics.note: Paraphrased. The firm-knuckleball approach and the two-speed idea are from the FanGraphs Dickey analysis.

spinAxis.value: Effectively none. The whole point is to kill rotation, and with almost no spin there is no stable axis and no meaningful Magnus force — which is why Statcast does not treat a knuckleball like a spinning pitch.

source.label: Wikipedia, Knuckleball

spinAxis.note: Inferred from the no-spin goal; the cited pages describe near-zero rotation, not a spin direction.

shape.value: No fixed direction and no settled shape — a late, sudden flutter that darts one way then another, unpredictable to the hitter, the catcher, and the pitcher alike. With the spin killed, the seams catch the air differently from instant to instant. It is famously almost as hard to catch as it is to hit.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Knuckleball (KN)

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured number. There is no fixed break direction — that unpredictability is the pitch.

teaching.value: Every other pitch in the atlas moves because it spins. The knuckleball moves because it doesn't. Kill the rotation, and the raised seams — whose position when the ball leaves the hand is never quite the same twice — catch the air and wander the ball off line. No spin, no Magnus, no two pitches alike.

source.label: Wikipedia, Knuckleball

teaching.note: Synthesis of the no-spin grip goal and the seam-drag movement mechanism.

display.heroIntro: Dig the fingernails in, brace the thumb, and push the ball flat with no spin. Strip away the rotation and the raised seams catch the air on their own, fluttering the ball late in any direction — almost as hard to catch as it is to hit.
display.foundationCaption: With the spin killed, there is almost no Magnus force: the ball sits on the spinless reference and the seams do the steering, erratically.
display.mastersIntro: Two documented knuckleballs, a generation apart. The visual is our own seam schematic; what sets each apart is in the read, not a gauge, and the movement is, by nature, not repeatable.

masterVariants.context: The only knuckleballer to win a Cy Young Award (2012), and the model of the hard, two-speed knuckleball that traded some flutter for less reaction time.

distinction.value: The firm, two-speed knuckleball: thrown with more pace than the classic floater, he gave up a little flutter to cut the hitter's reaction time, and changed speeds off it to keep them guessing.

source.label: FanGraphs, Tracking R.A. Dickey's Knuckleball

distinction.note: The firmer knuckleball, with a slower version off it — the read that defined his arm.

masterVariants.context: The modern knuckleballer who kept the pitch alive in the current game — and threw it more and more, the clearest proof that less rotation is the goal.

distinction.value: The knuckleball's survivor in the tracking era: he leaned on it harder season over season and pushed it to its quietest, slowest-turning extreme, where the ball barely rotates at all on its way to the plate.

source.label: FanGraphs, Matt Waldron and His Knuckleball Are Sticking Around

distinction.note: The read: he kept the pitch alive and chased the no-spin edge of it.

guide.tagline: Pushed with no spin so the seams catch the air and the ball wanders late. The catcher reads it about as well as the hitter does, which is to say barely.
guide.feel: Hold the ball off your palm on your fingernails. Push it flat toward the plate, no wrist, no spin. Stay smooth and slow.
steps: Dig your index and middle fingernails into the leather, thumb underneath for balance.
Keep the ball off your palm so the palm cannot add spin.
Push the ball toward the plate with an even arm, not a whip.
Let it leave with as little rotation as you can manage — the less it spins, the more it moves.

does.headline: It barely spins, so the stitches steer it.
does.plain: A knuckleball is thrown to not spin at all. Without spin there is nothing holding its path steady, so the raised stitches catch the moving air and nudge the ball one way, then another, late and unpredictably. Where it ends up is a mystery to everyone: the hitter, the catcher, even the pitcher who let it go.

grip.value: Almost any grip — usually an ordinary fastball or curve hold — thrown with drastically reduced arm speed on a high, exaggerated overhand lob. The grip barely matters: the pitch is defined by its trajectory and its speed, not by how the ball is held.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Eephus (EP)

grip.note: Paraphrased. MLB.com describes a pitch thrown very high in the air, resembling a slow-pitch softball trajectory; the overhand-lob delivery is the craft consensus.

gripDetails.value: MLB.com calls it, by design, the easiest pitch in baseball to hit — no unexpected movement, nothing on it. Its only weapon is the disruption: a hitter timed for heat has to wait on a pitch that floats in at a crawl.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Eephus (EP)

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. MLB.com: "it is the easiest pitch to hit in baseball" — the timing scramble is the point.

gripDetails.value: Oddly, it is not a no-spin pitch — it carries ordinary backspin, a touch more than a typical fastball. But thrown this slow, that spin barely matters. Gravity, not the spin, owns the flight.

source.label: James LeDoux, A Statcast study of the eephus pitch

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased from James LeDoux's Statcast study of tagged eephus pitches. Described as shape and feel, not a measured figure.

gripDetails.value: Rip Sewell invented it during World War II — the story ties it to a 1941 hunting accident — and the name comes from a teammate's line that "eephus ain't nothing, and that's a nothing pitch."

source.label: The Hardball Times, Rip Sewell, the Eephus, and the All-Star Game

gripDetails.note: Paraphrased. The Sewell origin and the Maurice Van Robays "nothing pitch" naming are documented in The Hardball Times.

orientation.knuckleLine: No canonical knuckle line — the hold is whatever the lob trusts.
orientation.palmFacing: Up and under the ball through the slow, exaggerated release.

provenance.value: No source supports one canonical eephus grip. The cited references describe an ordinary fastball or curve hold lobbed on a high arc — the pitch lives in the trajectory, not in the fingers.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Eephus (EP)

provenance.note: The grip stays undrawn on purpose: any fingers placed here would be invented geometry, and this atlas does not invent. Read the release cue instead.

gripModel.thumbRole: Thumb underneath for control; the grip is ordinary because the arc, not the spin, is the pitch.
gripModel.palmGapCue: Held loosely like a fastball or curve — almost any grip works.
gripModel.releaseCue: Throw it on a high, exaggerated overhand arc with drastically reduced arm speed, aiming to peak well above the batter and drop in.
gripModel.visualCaveat: Grip geometry is schematic and barely matters here; the eephus is defined by arc and timing, not grip.

mechanics.value: It is lobbed in at a small fraction of fastball pace on a towering rainbow that climbs well above head height before dropping into the zone on a steep angle. It is a once-a-game weapon: it works on timing, not stuff, and a hitter who is ready for it can crush it.

source.label: The Hardball Times, Rip Sewell, the Eephus, and the All-Star Game

mechanics.note: Paraphrased. The towering arc and crawling pace are described as shape and feel, per The Hardball Times.

spinAxis.value: A near-12:00 backspin orientation with little side tilt, so there is essentially no horizontal run and no platoon side. Inferred from the backspin it carries — and at this crawl the orientation hardly changes the flight anyway.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Eephus (EP)

spinAxis.note: Inferred from the backspin profile; no source publishes a spin-axis figure for the eephus.

shape.value: The shape is up, then down — a towering rainbow, not a sideways dart. The ball climbs high above head height between mound and plate, hangs there, then falls into the strike zone on a steep descending angle. The arc and the hang time are the whole pitch; nothing about it is late or sudden.

source.label: The Hardball Times, Rip Sewell, the Eephus, and the All-Star Game

shape.note: Described as shape, not a measured arc height. The towering climb and the steep drop are the read.

teaching.value: Every other pitch in the atlas uses spin to move or to resist gravity. The eephus does neither — it surrenders to gravity. Lobbed in at a fraction of fastball pace on a high arc, it hangs in the air far longer than anything a hitter has timed, and the hardest thing in hitting is recalibrating to a pitch that floats in slow and late.

source.label: MLB.com Statcast Glossary, Eephus (EP)

teaching.note: Synthesis of the MLB glossary's timing-disruption framing and the gravity-driven trajectory.

display.heroIntro: Lob it. The eephus floats in on a high arc at a fraction of fastball pace, peaking well above the batter before dropping into the zone. Its only weapon is time. The easiest pitch in baseball to hit, and one of the hardest to time.
display.foundationCaption: It carries normal backspin, but thrown this slow, gravity shapes the giant arc the plot cannot draw. Spin barely figures in.
display.mastersIntro: Two eephus throwers, eighty years apart: the man who invented it, and the modern artist who still dares it. The visual is our own seam schematic. What sets each version apart lives in the read, not a gauge.

masterVariants.context: The originator. His "folly floater," born in wartime, became a genuine weapon — until the one man who solved it did so on the biggest stage.

distinction.value: The towering wartime original — a sky-high rainbow that hitters of the era simply could not handle, until Ted Williams stepped forward and homered off it in the 1946 All-Star Game, the only home run ever hit off the pitch.

source.label: The Hardball Times, Rip Sewell, the Eephus, and the All-Star Game

distinction.note: The Sewell origin and the lone Williams home run are documented in The Hardball Times — sourced biography, not pitch behavior.

masterVariants.context: The modern keeper of the flame: a cerebral pitcher who will drop an eephus into the zone when the moment calls for it — even when he did not quite plan to.

distinction.value: The thinking man's version — a crawling lob dropped in for a called strike, slower than anything the modern game expects, deployed with a chess player's timing rather than as a novelty.

guide.tagline: A high, slow rainbow at a crawl. Its only weapon is throwing off a hitter's timing.
guide.feel: Lob it on a high arc with way less arm speed than a fastball. Aim to peak above the batter and drop it into the top of the zone.
steps: Take any ordinary grip — a fastball or curve hold is fine.
Throw it overhand on an exaggerated high arc, with drastically reduced arm speed.
Aim for the ball to peak well above the hitter and fall into the strike zone.
Pick your spot: it works once in a while, when a hitter is sitting dead-red, and gets crushed if they are ready.

does.headline: It floats in so slowly that timing falls apart.
does.plain: An eephus is a giant, slow rainbow, lobbed in at a fraction of fastball pace. It does not dart or dive. It just hangs in the air much longer than a hitter expects, then drops into the zone. A batter geared up for heat has to wait, and waiting is the hardest thing to do at the plate.
